To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Maya Patel <finance@apexmed.com>
Date: Wed, 2026-09-08T09:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Procure/Billing follow-up from today's call

Hi Ava — thanks for the clear recap. From AP's side: 

- PO cadence: ApexMed will issue a monthly consolidated PO (one PO per month covering cloud vendor spend). That PO number must appear on any invoice we pay against.
- Payment method: PO + ACH (we don't put large vendor spend on a corporate card).
- Net terms: our standard is net 60 for enterprise vendors; exceptions require procurement lead approval. We'll request 60, but confirmers will be AP (Maya) and Procurement.
- Invoice format: PDF is fine. Our AP system can ingest a single PDF per month. We do not currently use XML/EDI for cloud vendor invoices.
- Billing address & Tax ID: we'll need the legal billing address and Redwood's EIN. Also will require a completed vendor form and W9.
- Marketplace: Procurement prefers direct vendor relationships for now; not opposed to marketplace in long term, but not a blocker for this procurement.

I'll loop in Maya for vendor form details. Please send the W9 and the vendor contact details.

Jordan Hughes
Head of IT Procurement | ApexMed
jordan.hughes@apexmed.com | (212) 555-0110

preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.com>, Sam Torres <sam.torres@atlascloud.com>
Cc: procurement@atlascloud.com
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Readiness alignment: scrub, ownership, and exception escrow

Team / Sam —

Quick alignment ahead of next-week activation window. We ran a brief readiness scrub with Product and Docs yesterday; three key asks emerged that I want to land before Friday so AtlasCloud can keep the Sept 20 activation:

1) Ownership for the two enablement flows (admin onboarding + runtime tuning) — need clear owners and training slots.
2) Exception escrow proposal — we sketched a 72-hour delay escrow for non-blocking platform items (docs, minor infra tweaks) with a roll-back trigger if SLA-affecting items remain. Need legal/Security OK and an internal sign-off process.
3) Final enablement deck and a single source-of-truth for rollback/runbook.

I attached a working draft of the enablement deck and the template for the exception escrow. Please read the escrow template and call out any non-starters.

Asks: can each of you confirm (A) who will own the enablement lane, (B) three candidate training slots next week, and (C) any absolute no-go items for the escrow? Sam / Procurement — if there are procurement timelines we should bake into the escrow, flag them.

Attachments: enablement-deck-v2.pdf (slides), exception-escrow-template.xlsx (stub)

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Launch PM, Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com

-----
From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.com>, Sam Torres <sam.torres@atlascloud.com>
Cc: procurement@atlascloud.com
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Readiness alignment: scrub, ownership, and exception escrow

Ava — thanks. Quick notes from enablement angle:

- I can own admin onboarding lane (Marissa). Proposed owner for runtime tuning: Ben (unless he prefers not to).
- Training slots (US-friendly): Wed 9/15 10:00-11:30 PT, Thu 9/16 14:00-15:30 PT, Mon 9/20 09:00-10:30 PT. Each slot has 45m presentation + 30m hands-on.
- Absolute no-go for escrow: any window that allows shipping an unscoped data retention change or bypasses encryption checks. Those must be triaged into a blocking path.

I've commented in the Drive doc (link below) on slide 6 where we need a short troubleshooting checklist and on slide 10 for the RBAC mapping example.

Marissa Cole
Developer Relations / Enablement
marissa@redwood.com

To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.com>, Sam Torres <sam.torres@atlascloud.com>

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — the team is excited about faster inference for our triage workflows. We want to lock an initial mutual action plan so engineering and product ops can coordinate timelines. High level asks from our side:

- Go-live target: 2026-11-15 (soft launch with 10% traffic)
- Must-haves: embeddings + generation API calls with < 200ms P95 on short queries
- Compliance/retention: need clear guidance on event logs retention and isolation for PHI-adjacent data

Can you send a MAP draft that maps milestones, owners, and a recommended sprint cadence? If you can include obvious risk lanes (infra, security approval, integration hooks) that would help our PM.

Jane

title: Clarify consumption vs first-license surcharge for private VPC deployment
preview:
From: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
Date: Tue, 01 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Hi Ava / Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Procurement at Mediq asked for a short write-up about how your pricing works for an on-prem (VPC-hosted) installation — specifically two items: 1) how the first-license "consumption buffer" surcharge works versus ongoing metered consumption, and 2) whether the platform fee is a one-time onboarding surcharge or a recurring monthly charge. They also asked whether we can cap the platform surcharge for the first year.

Could you send a two-line summary that I can drop into our procurement packet? Also attaching the draft SOW we have — we'd like to finalize commercial terms before their Q4 budget approval.

Best,
Jordan
CISO, Mediq Health

Attachment: Mediq_SOW_v2.pdf (application/pdf, 1.1MB)

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Jordan — quick response below and I can follow up with more detail. Short summary for procurement: 

- First-license consumption buffer is a one-time provisioning surcharge (covers reserved capacity, image packaging, and a 30-day burst buffer). It is invoiced on signing and capped at the agreed amount in the SOW.
- Platform fee is recurring (monthly) and covers control-plane, observability retention, and patch orchestration; we can provide a discounted ramp in months 1–12 for customers committing to a 12+ month support tier.

If you want a single-line option to paste: "Initial provisioning surcharge (one-time) + recurring platform fee (monthly), with a first-year ramp discount available under a 12-month commitment."

Two quick clarifications to help negotiations: the "consumption buffer" is not usage billing — after the first 30 days, consumption is metered separately (per-token / per-embedding or per-node-hour depending on chosen licensing). Also, platform fee discounts are typically structured as % off for months 1–12 tied to a minimum committed node count.

I can join a call with procurement Thursday if helpful.

— Ava
Director, Sales Engineering
Redwood Inference

Attachment: license_matrix_ava_review.xlsx (spreadsheet, 120KB)

From: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ethan Rhodes <legal@mediq.io>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 08:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Thanks Ava — that helps. Procurement asks two follow-ups: (A) can Mediq get a firm cap on total first-year platform spend (provisioning surcharge + 12 months of recurring platform fee) so they can include it in their 2027 capital ask, and (B) how do support tiers map to response times and severity definitions (we need a table to include in the procurement packet). Legal will want any cap reflected as an appendix in the SOW.

Can you provide a capped number range (ballpark okay) and the support-tier bullets by EOD Friday?

Thanks,
Lina
Senior Procurement Manager, Mediq Health

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 13:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Lina — draft numbers below (ballpark ranges assuming 3-node private pool, Standard support and a modest token profile):

- One-time provisioning surcharge (consumption buffer): $45k — $60k (covers packaging, IP escrow prep, initial 30-day burst).
- Recurring platform fee (monthly): $6k — $9k per month.
- First-year capped spend (worst case): $117k — $168k (surcharge + 12 months platform fee).

Support tiers (summary):
- Standard: Business hours (M–F, 9–5) support, 8–24 hour response depending on Sev; patching windows scheduled monthly.
- Enhanced: 24/7 on-call for Sev1, 4-hour response for Sev1, 8-hour for Sev2, quarterly health checks and 8 hours of migration assistance.
- Enterprise: Dedicated TAM, guaranteed 2-hour Sev1 response, weekly ops reviews, unlimited runbook updates, and 40 hours of onboarding engineering.

Notes: We can convert first-license surcharge to a 12-month amortized credit if Mediq signs a 12+ month committed contract and pre-pays 6 months platform fee; we can include that as an appendix to the SOW.

Happy to convert these ranges into fixed-line items for the SOW appendix once you confirm node count and desired support tier.

— Marissa
Head of Commercials, Redwood Inference

From: Ethan Rhodes <legal@mediq.io>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Marissa / Ava — Legal reviewed and we like the amortization clause idea. Request two edits for the SOW appendix: (1) explicit definition of "provisioning surcharge" line items (engineering hours, packaging, escrow) with a not-to-exceed figure, and (2) a clear SLA table tying support tiers to response times and escalation contacts.

If you can send a redlined appendix by Wednesday we can fast-track our internal approval. Also confirm whether license transfer on hardware failure is included or priced separately.

I can join a call with procurement Thursday if helpful.

To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 15:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Quick finance take: 
- Redemption window: 90 days is acceptable. We should document the exact trigger (offer activation date) and include a clause for exceptions with exec sign-off.
- Crediting treatment: co-sell credits will be recorded as contra-revenue. Frontloading 60/40 split is workable, but we need to ensure the partner-side payment terms (how CloudResell will be paid/credited) are explicit. If CloudResell expects a cash payout versus invoice offset, that changes our cash flow timing and requires treasury sign-off.
- SKU mapping: fine at product-family level. Please ensure the mapping includes the GCP SKU IDs (not just names) so attribution is granular enough for Google reports.
- Attachments: I'll need the final private_offer_terms.pdf and the PO from Acme to validate invoice triggers.

I'll be on the call at 10am PT tomorrow.

Ben Carter
Finance — Partnerships Billing


From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: CloudResell Ops <cpo@cloudresell.com>, Iris Wang <iris@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>, Procurement <procurement@acme.ai>
Date: Fri, 04 Sep 2026 16:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting — proposed schedule and next steps

Hi Samir / Iris — thanks for the draft. After internal review here is Redwood's proposed approach for Acme's $500k commit (deal RDW-2026-0047):

1) Redemption window: 90 days from private-offer activation.
2) Drawdown cadence (recommended):
   - Month 0 (activation): frontload 25% of expected committed units as a credit pool to CloudResell to enable early onboarding (up to $125k of usage value).
   - Months 1–2: additional 35% distributed across two months (~17.5% each).
   - Months 3–6: remaining 40% amortized evenly.
3) Crediting split: 60% of partner-eligible credit delivered in first two months (per Marissa's hybrid plan), 40% amortized months 3–6.
4) SKU mapping: Marissa attached sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx — please populate GCP SKU IDs where possible. We'll accept product-family mapping but GCP requires SKU IDs for attribution.
5) Invoicing / offsets: Redwood will reconcile monthly usage and apply partner credits as contra-revenue on invoices to CloudResell. If CloudResell requires cash payouts, we need a separate payout agreement and treasury approval.

Next steps:
- Samir: confirm that CloudResell can accept a 90-day redemption window and the frontload numbers above.
- Iris / Acme Procurement: please send the PO for the committed amount or confirm you will accept the private offer activation flow. If you can email PO to procurement@redwood.ai that will speed finance validation.
- Samir: if agreed, please route the final private offer through DocuSign (link recommended below) and attach signed MOP when complete.

We are happy to join a brief sync to finalize mapping and cadence. Proposed slot: Fri 9/4 10am PT (Jordan, Ben, Marissa, Alex available).

Best,
Alex Martinez
Director, GTM Partnerships
Redwood Inference

Hi Ava,

Super-brief background:
- Use case: customer support knowledge base search + assistant (embeddings + reranking).
- Team: AI platform + product (2 engineers, 1 ML infra).
- Timeline: hoping to kick a pilot in early September and evaluate for a Q4 production push.

Head of AI, Marketly
ops@marketly.ai

Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T17:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Interested in Redwood — quick demo?

Hi Tim — great to meet you and glad you signed up. I'm Ava on the solutions team; I'll run point for the demo and intake. A couple quick questions so we tailor the session and make the time valuable:

1) Primary objective: is the priority retrieval relevance (search) or multi-turn assistant behavior? If both, which is higher priority?
2) Estimated monthly volume: approximate API requests or token counts you expect during a pilot and at production scale?
3) Peak concurrency: any SLA/latency targets we should know about (e.g., sub-100ms p50 for short queries)?
4) Timeline: you mentioned early September pilot — is that a two-week pilot or longer evaluation window?
5) Security/compliance: do you need SSO (Okta/SAML), specific data residency, or KMS/HSM integrations for keys?
6) Sandbox: what data sample would be most useful (KB dump, CSV of tickets, vector index snapshot)?

Proposed slots for a 30-minute demo + intake call (PT):
- Thu Sep 2 — 11:00 AM PT
- Thu Sep 2 — 2:00 PM PT
- Fri Sep 3 — 10:00 AM PT

You can also pick a time directly from my calendar: https://cal.redwoodinference.com/ava/book

I've attached a short one-pager (redwood-one-pager.pdf) and a tiny intake form link (demo-intake-form-link.txt) to accelerate the technical prep — if you can fill the form ahead of the call we'll be able to surface a tailored demo.

If you prefer, I can loop in Ben (cc'd) for any deeper infra / throughput questions.

Best,
Ava
SDR, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com

Thanks, Ava — answers below and happy to take the 11am PT slot on Sep 2.

- Primary objective: search relevance first (we want help surfacing exact KB sections), secondarily a short assistant for triage.
- Pilot volume: roughly 40k requests / month in pilot, ~1.2M requests / month at scale (ballpark).
- Tokens: pilot ~120k tokens/month, production ~3.5M tokens/month.
- Peak concurrency: expect short bursts up to ~250 concurrent requests during business hours.
- Timeline: target pilot kickoff the week of Sep 6 (2–4 week pilot), production evaluation through Q4.
- Security: Okta SSO required for console access; would like KMS support for customer-managed keys long term.
- Sandbox: a 10–15k ticket KB dump (CSV) would be ideal so our engineers can run latency checks; sample vector index also helpful.

Please go ahead and book Sep 2 at 11:00 AM PT. Also, Ben — can you confirm if a small sandbox index is something we can provision for Marketly ahead of the meeting?

—Tim

Head of AI, Marketly

title: Capacity architecture & activation options for QuantumBridge
preview:
From: David Park <david.park@quantumbridge.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@quantumbridge.ai>, procurement@quantumbridge.ai
Date: 2026-08-22T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: sizing discussion follow-up

Alex — thanks again for the demo this morning. QuantumBridge is excited about the latency profile and the KV cache savings you showed. Before we move to legal/procurement, procurement asked for a clear set of options for committed capacity: a) a conservative minimum-guarantee (to match our predictable inference peaks), b) a phased ramp (3-month onramp plan), and c) an aggressive committed+burst plan for Q4. Can you send a short doc/worksheet with cluster sizes, minimum GPU floors, expected p99 latency at typical batch sizes, and rough monthly committed cost for each option? Would also like to understand fallback behavior if dedicated capacity is saturated. —David

Attachments: qb-initial-load-patterns.csv (stub)

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: David Park <david.park@quantumbridge.ai>, Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@quantumbridge.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-22T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: sizing discussion follow-up

David — good request. I'm attaching a short worksheet that lays out three architecture options and the activation timelines. High level: 1) Baseline (min-guarantee): 8-GPU lane (A40/80 equiv) with autoscaling to 16 for bursts; target p99 ~220ms for 1k-token avg prompts when batching is enabled; monthly committed cost estimate $62k. 2) Phased Ramp: start at 4 GPUs for month 1, add 8 GPUs month 2, reach 16 by month 3; this lowers initial spend and lets us tune batching/quantization thresholds; effective committed floor when fully ramped: 16 GPUs; average p99 180–200ms at higher batch efficiency. 3) Committed+Burst: 24 reserved GPUs with a separate burst pool (ephemeral) up to 40 GPUs for short windows — suitable if you have Q4 spikes. Bursting behavior: when dedicated pool is saturated, we can route low-priority traffic to a compatible hosted instance (warm standby model) or soft-throttle/queue with SLA-grade notifications.

I've summarized assumptions inside the sheet (attached). Two asks for you: confirm target p99 budget (what latency SLA matters for user-facing inference vs async tasks), and share an expected token distribution (short/med/long) so we can refine kernel selection and the lane sizing.

I copied Ben to provide commercial numbers and Laura for activation sequencing.

Attachments: qb-capacity-options-v1.xlsx (spreadsheet)

—Alex Martinez
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: David Park <david.park@quantumbridge.ai>
Cc: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-23T08:27:00-07:00
Subject: Re: sizing discussion follow-up

David — quick commercial note to align expectations. The spreadsheet shows committed monthly pricing per GPU-equivalent and a blended discount based on 12-month vs 36-month term. For the Baseline (8-GPU floor) the monthly committed price is $62k as Alex noted (includes reserved compute + baseline support). Phased Ramp (4 -> 8 -> 16) carries a pro-rated ramp credit in months 1–3; fully ramped committed spend is ~ $112k/mo. Committed+Burst is priced at $160k/mo committed with burst capacity billed separately at usage rates; we can include a capped burst budget for predictability.

If procurement wants a safety clause: suggest adding an automatic capacity review at 90 days and a one-time adjust within the first 120 days to realign floors with actual traffic (min 30-day notice before reductions). Happy to run a short TCO comparison vs hosted-only for your expected workload if you share the token profile.

—Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@quantumbridge.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@quantumbridge.ai, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-24T10:10:00-07:00
Subject: Re: capacity architecture & activation options for QuantumBridge

Thanks Alex and Ben — the worksheet helps. A couple of follow-ups from our end: 1) Can you spell out how autoscaling interacts with committed floors? For example: if we reserve an 8-GPU lane and autoscaling is enabled to 16, what portion of that autoscale is guaranteed vs burst (and how is it billed)? 2) For the phased ramp option, procurement would like explicit activation milestones and acceptance criteria (e.g., performance verification on their eval prompts, key rotation and audit logs in place). 3) We need a fallback SLO: if dedicated lane falls below p99 SLA because of saturation, what is the typical fallback sequence and expected user impact windows?

Also looping Rishi from Redwood for technical confirmation on kernel/quantization compatibility and to confirm whether we can meet encryption & KMS requirements during activation.

—Elena
Head of Platform Engineering, QuantumBridge

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@quantumbridge.ai>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-25T16:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: capacity architecture & activation options for QuantumBridge

Elena — quick technical notes to address your points: 1) Autoscaling vs guaranteed floor: reserved lane (8 GPUs) is the guaranteed capacity; autoscaling pool is configured as part of the deployment policy. We support two autoscale bands: a reserved-anchored autoscale (where additional instances are still billed under the committed contract at a discounted overage rate) and a purely ephemeral burst (billed at on-demand rates). For your procurement template we can set the reserved-anchored autoscale to up to +100% (so 8 -> 16) with an overage discount tier (e.g., 40% off on-demand) for predictable cost. 2) Activation milestones for phased ramp: (a) Week 0–2: provisioning and baseline tests (connectivity, KMS, audit log flow); (b) Week 3–6: 4 GPU lane active, ingest test traffic, run evaluation set; (c) Week 7–10: expand to 8 GPUs, validate latency/p99 with production sampling; (d) Week 11–14: expand to 16 GPUs, finalize canary rollout and retention policies. Each milestone ends with a short sign-off checklist (performance targets, security checklist, SLO smoke tests). 3) Fallback sequence: first, we direct low-priority or async requests to the hosted fallback model variant (same model family, slightly higher latency and lower batching efficiency). Second, if that is insufficient, we apply short-lived queueing with progressive retry—this preserves high-priority user flows. Typical user impact window for fallback events is 30–120 seconds to transition and warm the fallback; we include real-time alerts and a traffic-split rollback if degradation exceeds configurable thresholds.

I attached a small archive with example eval traces and a minimal activation checklist. Happy to sync on a 30-min call to walk procurement through the milestone language if helpful.

Attachments: quantumbridge-eval-traces.zip (archive, stub)

—Rishi Malhotra
Director, Serving Runtime, Redwood Inference

Cc: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-23T08:27:00-07:00
Subject: Re: sizing discussion follow-up

David — quick commercial note to align expectations. The spreadsheet shows committed monthly pricing per GPU-equivalent and a blended discount based on 12-month vs 36-month term. For the Baseline (8-GPU floor) the monthly committed price is $62k as Alex noted (includes reserved compute + baseline support). Phased Ramp (4 -> 8 -> 16) carries a pro-rated ramp credit in months 1–3; fully ramped committed spend is ~ $112k/mo. Committed+Burst is priced at $160k/mo committed with burst capacity billed separately at usage rates; we can include a capped burst budget for predictability.

If procurement wants a safety clause: suggest adding an automatic capacity review at 90 days and a one-time adjust within the first 120 days to realign floors with actual traffic (min 30-day notice before reductions). Happy to run a short TCO comparison vs hosted-only for your expected workload if you share the token profile.

—Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@quantumbridge.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@quantumbridge.ai, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-24T10:10:00-07:00
Subject: Re: capacity architecture & activation options for QuantumBridge

Thanks Alex and Ben — the worksheet helps. A couple of follow-ups from our end: 1) Can you spell out how autoscaling interacts with committed floors? For example: if we reserve an 8-GPU lane and autoscaling is enabled to 16, what portion of that autoscale is guaranteed vs burst (and how is it billed)? 2) For the phased ramp option, procurement would like explicit activation milestones and acceptance criteria (e.g., performance verification on their eval prompts, key rotation and audit logs in place). 3) We need a fallback SLO: if dedicated lane falls below p99 SLA because of saturation, what is the typical fallback sequence and expected user impact windows?

Also looping Rishi from Redwood for technical confirmation on kernel/quantization compatibility and to confirm whether we can meet encryption & KMS requirements during activation.

—Elena
Head of Platform Engineering, QuantumBridge

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@quantumbridge.ai>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-25T16:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: capacity architecture & activation options for QuantumBridge

Elena — quick technical notes to address your points: 1) Autoscaling vs guaranteed floor: reserved lane (8 GPUs) is the guaranteed capacity; autoscaling pool is configured as part of the deployment policy. We support two autoscale bands: a reserved-anchored autoscale (where additional instances are still billed under the committed contract at a discounted overage rate) and a purely ephemeral burst (billed at on-demand rates). For your procurement template we can set the reserved-anchored autoscale to up to +100% (so 8 -> 16) with an overage discount tier (e.g., 40% off on-demand) for predictable cost. 2) Activation milestones for phased ramp: (a) Week 0–2: provisioning and baseline tests (connectivity, KMS, audit log flow); (b) Week 3–6: 4 GPU lane active, ingest test traffic, run evaluation set; (c) Week 7–10: expand to 8 GPUs, validate latency/p99 with production sampling; (d) Week 11–14: expand to 16 GPUs, finalize canary rollout and retention policies. Each milestone ends with a short sign-off checklist (performance targets, security checklist, SLO smoke tests). 3) Fallback sequence: first, we direct low-priority or async requests to the hosted fallback model variant (same model family, slightly higher latency and lower batching efficiency). Second, if that is insufficient, we apply short-lived queueing with progressive retry—this preserves high-priority user flows. Typical user impact window for fallback events is 30–120 seconds to transition and warm the fallback; we include real-time alerts and a traffic-split rollback if degradation exceeds configurable thresholds.

I attached a small archive with example eval traces and a minimal activation checklist. Happy to sync on a 30-min call to walk procurement through the milestone language if helpful.

Attachments: quantumbridge-eval-traces.zip (archive, stub)

—Rishi Malhotra
Director, Serving Runtime, Redwood Inference

title: Atlas eval: prioritized throughput & p95/p99 action plan
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Priya Menon <priya@atlascloud.com>, Rahul Sharma <rahul@atlascloud.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-22T09:15:00-07:00
Subject: Atlas eval: follow-up from today's call & suggested throughput tests

Hi Priya / Rahul,

Thanks for the time today — helpful to hear your SLOs and the key customer scenarios. Quick recap + a narrow set of tests we recommend to make the hyperscaler comparison defensible: 

Priority summary (from our call):
- Target latency SLO: p95 < 120ms for short chat turns (~64 tokens)
- Long-form background generation: p95 < 800ms for 512-token responses
- Cost sensitivity: prefer tests that reflect tokens/sec and per-token cost at 95th percentile

Proposed focused A/B tests (we’ll run Redwood hosted vs Hyperscaler A API using the same model family):
1) Short-seq single-client: 64-token prompts, 1000 warmup requests, 10k measured requests; measure p50/p95/p99, tokens/sec, tail latency under steady-state.
2) Long-seq burst: 512-token outputs with 20 concurrent connections to emulate parallel UX flows; measure throughput (tokens/sec) and p99 under bursts.
3) Batching sensitivity: run same short-seq at batch sizes [1, 4, 8] to show latency vs cost tradeoffs.
4) KV cache probe: for chat-like flows, measure p95 with and without prefix/KV caching to show savings in tokens/sec and latency.

Deliverables we’ll provide: test harness config, raw CSVs, per-route p50/p95/p99 plots, and a concise recommendation slide deck. Ben will own the lab run; Karthik will validate kernel/quantization parity vs Hyperscaler profiles.

Can you confirm the most realistic production request mix (ratio short:long:multi-turn) and any constraints on example prompts/PII so we can use representative prompts? Also confirm whether you want us to run tests in us-west or your eu region.

Attaching a short eval proposal workbook and the test instructions we plan to execute.

Next steps (proposed):
- Priya: confirm mix and region by EOD Wednesday 8/25
- Redwood (Ben/Karthik): lock test infra and start dry runs on 8/26
- Full A/B runs target week of 8/31

Thanks — happy to tighten scope if you prefer a smaller starter test.

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference

Attachments: atlas-eval-proposal.xlsx, throughput-test-instructions.md


From: Priya Menon <priya@atlascloud.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Rahul Sharma <rahul@atlascloud.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-22T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Atlas eval: follow-up from today's call & suggested throughput tests

Thanks Ava — that plan looks good and nicely scoped. A few clarifications from our side: 

1) Request mix: short:long:multi-turn = 60:20:20 (short = < 128 tokens, long = 256-512, multi-turn = 4–6 messages with short turn lengths).
2) Region: run tests in us-west first (we'll consider eu later).
3) Prompts: we will provide a sanitized set of 30 representative prompts (no PII) by end of day Tuesday 8/25.
4) Hyperscaler target: please include Hyperscaler A and Hyperscaler B (their managed LLM endpoints) in the comparison — both are what we currently evaluate.

Important callout: our procurement team will want clear p95/p99 snapshots and cost-per-1000-tokens for each run — the more reproducible the harness the better. Also, if you can include a short section that explains where Redwood's batching + KV cache impacts unit cost versus the Hyperscalers, that will be useful for our CFO.

We’re OK with the proposed schedule. Confirm if Redwood needs an IP or security checklist completed before we hand you the prompts.

Regards,
Priya Menon
Head of Platform
Atlas Cloud


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-23T08:42:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Atlas eval - infra & test notes (internal)

Looping internal notes here so we have an execution checklist. Priya asked for Hyperscaler A & B; we should be explicit about run parity. 

Execution checklist (Ben owner):
- Provision identical VM/GPU shapes in us-west for fairness (instances + network egress as close as possible) — target done by 8/25.
- Use same model weights (open/checksum) and tokenization to avoid model mismatch noise. Where Hyperscaler provides only API, instrument at request/response level to capture latency and token counts.
- Measurement: p50/p95/p99 (latency), tokens/sec, CPU/GPU util, and cost-per-1000-tokens estimated from run usage.
- Batching: implement batch plumbing with sizes [1,4,8] and fixed timeouts to reflect production routing.
- KV cache: two test modes (cold start vs warmed prefix cache). We'll include scripts to preload cache.

Karthik — can you validate that our quantized runtime path produces comparable output to the float baseline for the short test prompts? If the outputs diverge substantively we need to flag quality/regression risk to Priya.

Also: record the exact SDK versions, driver/kernel versions and attach logs so procurement has an audit trail.

Ben
Principal Performance Engineer
Redwood Inference

Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>

- Test set: Redwood harness vs Hyperscaler APIs using the same sanitized prompt set you’ll provide

- 8/25 EOD: Atlas shares sanitized prompts (Priya)
- 8/26: Infra provisioned & dry runs (Ben)
- 8/27–8/29: Full A/B runs + KV cache permutations (Ben / Redwood lab)

Deliverables:

title: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency
preview:
From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Cc: timothy.holt@acmehealth.com
Date: 2026-08-20 09:12 -0700
Subject: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Team —

Monitoring alerted at 03:08 UTC to a sustained p95 tail latency increase for model alpha-9 serving on our hosted fleet. Summary: p95 has drifted from ~420ms baseline to 1.05s over the last 4 hours (traffic-weighted), with increased variance on longer prompts. The spike correlates with the alpha-9 rollout 02:45 UTC and a kernel change in the optimized attention path.

Quick findings (attachments included):
- 03:00–06:00 UTC: gradual ramp, 06:00–09:00 UTC: step increase coincident with a subset of requests hitting the new kv-cache path.
- Affected tenants: 12 customers in US region; Acme Health shows elevated client-side timeouts on 0.7% of requests (attached sample traces).
- Reproduction locally on a sample workload shows p95 ~1.1s vs alpha-8 p95 ~430ms.

Mitigation options (tradeoffs):
1) Immediate: \"pin\" affected tenants to alpha-8 variant (no model code change) and apply a temporary traffic cap for alpha-9 for those tenants (recommended cap: 60% routing to alpha-9). Low-risk for correctness, moderate customer-visible behavior change.
2) Rollback alpha-9 globally to alpha-8 (broad rollback). Fast but disruptive to rollout metrics and to other tenants already benefiting from alpha-9 improvements.
3) Kernel patch hotfix (engineering) — likely takes 6+ hours to validate and deploy.

Request: I need authorization from sales/ops leadership to enact option #1 for Acme Health and the most impacted tenants as an emergency mitigation while we investigate. This will temporarily alter routing (affects latency SLOs coverage and may trigger an SLA exception for impacted time window).

Attachments: alpha9-tail-traces.tar.gz, alpha9-profiles.csv

If approved, I will: (a) pin Acme Health to alpha-8, (b) set traffic cap to 60% for alpha-9 for the next 12 hours, (c) continue trace collection and produce a short hotfix plan.

Rishi
Rishi Malhotra, Senior SRE
Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20 09:34 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Thanks Rishi — this looks urgent. A couple quick Qs before I loop in legal/finance if needed:
- Can you confirm whether pinning to alpha-8 will change model outputs for any of Acme Health's anchored prompts (we need to avoid regressions for their clinical scoring flows)?
- Do we have a precise list of the 12 tenants (I only want to authorize for the small set that are showing client-side timeouts) — please paste IDs in a follow-up.
- I prefer option #1 if delta in outputs is minimal. Ben — can you pre-approve an emergency routing policy for Acme Health for a 2-hour mitigation window while we triage? We can extend if necessary but want an initial guardrail.

Ava Chen
Account Executive — Enterprise
Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0182


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20 10:05 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Approve option #1 for Acme Health and the other tenants with confirmed client-side timeouts. Conditions:
- Keep the initial window to 2 hours and notify the customer immediately (Lauren, please send a short note).
- If the mitigation is still required after 2 hours, escalate back to Ben/Finance for a formal SLA exception.

Ava — you have my sign-off for the emergency routing policy for ACME-2345 for the 2-hour mitigation window. Rishi — proceed with pin + 60% cap for those tenant IDs once you paste them.

Ben Carter
Sales — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
To: timothy.holt@acmehealth.com
Cc: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-20 10:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency

Timothy —

We're aware of increased tail latency impacting a small percentage of inference calls made by Acme Health this morning. Our engineering team has identified the likely cause and proposed a short mitigation that reduces exposure while they finish a fix. With your permission we'll:
- Temporarily route Acme Health traffic to the previous model variant for a short window, and
- Apply a conservative traffic cap to the new variant to reduce the chance of timeouts on your production calls.

We expect this to be a brief measure (initially 2 hours). Will this window work for your on-call/team? If you'd prefer we can schedule a similar maintenance window instead.

Apologies for the disruption — we'll follow up with a post-mortem and traces.

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference
lauren_bishop@redwood.com


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Cc: timothy.holt@acmehealth.com
Date: 2026-08-21 14:28 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency — actions taken and next steps

Update and actions taken:
- 2026-08-20 10:45 PDT: Pinned Acme Health and 4 other impacted tenant IDs to alpha-8.
- 2026-08-20 10:50 PDT: Applied traffic cap of 60% routing to alpha-9 for the affected tenant group.
- 2026-08-20 11:30 PDT: Confirmed client-side timeouts for Acme Health fell from 0.7% -> 0.02% on the sampled window. p95 for their sampled calls dropped to ~460ms.
- Continued trace capture stored to Drive (link below). Attached: proposed-traffic-cap.docx, alpha9-profiles.csv.

Recommendation: Keep the pin + cap in place until we validate the kernel patch. If alpha-9 p95 persists above 800ms after the patch window, I recommend a staged rollback for alpha-9 rollout cohorts rather than a global rollback. Please confirm if you authorize me to maintain the mitigation through 23:59 PST tonight and to initiate staged rollback gating if the patch does not show improvement within the next maintenance window.

Thanks,
Rishi Malhotra
Senior SRE — Runtime Reliability
rishi_malhotra@redwood.com
Attachments: proposed-traffic-cap.docx, alpha9-profiles.csv

We're aware of increased tail latency impacting a small percentage of inference calls made by Acme Health this morning. Our engineering team has identified the likely cause and proposed a short mitigation that reduces exposure while they finish a fix. With your permission we'll:
- Temporarily route Acme Health traffic to the previous model variant for a short window, and
- Apply a conservative traffic cap to the new variant to reduce the chance of timeouts on your production calls.

We expect this to be a brief measure (initially 2 hours). Will this window work for your on-call/team? If you'd prefer we can schedule a similar maintenance window instead.

Apologies for the disruption — we'll follow up with a post-mortem and traces.

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference
lauren_bishop@redwood.com


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Cc: timothy.holt@acmehealth.com
Date: 2026-08-21 14:28 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: authorize emergency model pin & traffic cap for alpha-9 tail latency — actions taken and next steps

Update and actions taken:
- 2026-08-20 10:45 PDT: Pinned Acme Health and 4 other impacted tenant IDs to alpha-8.
- 2026-08-20 10:50 PDT: Applied traffic cap of 60% routing to alpha-9 for the affected tenant group.
- 2026-08-20 11:30 PDT: Confirmed client-side timeouts for Acme Health fell from 0.7% -> 0.02% on the sampled window. p95 for their sampled calls dropped to ~460ms.
- Continued trace capture stored to Drive (link below). Attached: proposed-traffic-cap.docx, alpha9-profiles.csv.

Recommendation: Keep the pin + cap in place until we validate the kernel patch. If alpha-9 p95 persists above 800ms after the patch window, I recommend a staged rollback for alpha-9 rollout cohorts rather than a global rollback. Please confirm if you authorize me to maintain the mitigation through 23:59 PST tonight and to initiate staged rollback gating if the patch does not show improvement within the next maintenance window.

Thanks,
Rishi Malhotra
Senior SRE — Runtime Reliability
rishi_malhotra@redwood.com
Attachments: proposed-traffic-cap.docx, alpha9-profiles.csv

title: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com>, Priya Desai <priya@opsly.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)

Hi Tom / Priya — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap + next steps so we have a common plan.

Call highlights:
- Opsly latency expectations: p95 ~80ms for inference on typical 512-token merchant requests; p99 target currently not required but helpful to track.
- Burst profile: baseline steady-state ~120 QPS (peak hourly 200 QPS), but you saw short spikes up to ~600 QPS for ~20–45s during promotions.
- Security: standard SOC2 controls are required; VPC/private endpoints are a later ask (we discussed Dedicated + Private as options).
- Time-to-value: you want to get an integration live for the holiday pilot in 6 weeks if possible.

Preliminary guidance (what we discussed):
- Hosted API is the fastest path to start (low setup). It's a reasonable fit if your steady-state + short bursts can be handled by autoscaled hosted capacity and you accept a fallback policy for sustained multi-minute spikes. Hosted is great when burst durations are short (<1 minute) and overall p95 target is in the 50–150ms range.
- Dedicated capacity reduces unit cost for high sustained QPS and gives predictable tail performance for strict latency SLOs. It's the recommended path if you see repeated >2 minute spikes at 400–600 QPS or need guaranteed isolation (or want to combine with a Private deployment later).

Concrete next steps I propose: 
1) Opsly: share a 10–30 minute representative traffic trace (sample attached: pilot-qps-sample.csv) — include timestamps and QPS by second plus example request sizes.
2) Redwood: run a short bench against a Redwood-verified model variant (we'll run hosted and a dedicated-style simulation) and return a p50/p95/p99 latency table + throughput curve.
3) Redwood: provide quick cost sketch for a 2-week dedicated pilot (Ben will own) and a hosted fallback pattern to keep the integration live during ramp.
4) Opsly & Redwood: agree on a minimal pilot SLA and a go/no-go checkpoint after the bench.

I've attached a short SLO proposal (slo-proposal.pdf) that maps your p95 target to simple routing rules we can use during the pilot. If you can send the trace by EOD Thursday PST we'll aim to return bench numbers within 48 hours.

Thanks — I’ll coordinate infra for the bench once we have the trace.

—
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference
Attachments: pilot-qps-sample.csv (CSV), slo-proposal.pdf (PDF)

From: Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Desai <priya@opsly.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 15:47:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)

Thanks Ava — this is super helpful. I'm attaching a 12-minute trace (high-traffic window from last promo) that includes per-second QPS and payload sizes.

Two quick clarifications: 
- The 600 QPS spikes are usually ~30–40s, but we saw one ~90s spike last quarter.
- Security: our security team will push for a VPC/private option for production, but for the pilot they are willing to accept hosted endpoints with additional audit logging.

Can you also ballpark the difference in monthly cost between a hosted ramp and a small dedicated pool for the pilot so we can flag this to finance? No need for a detailed quote — a rough delta is fine.

Tom
CTO, Opsly
Attachments: opsly-20260612-trace.json (JSON)

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 16:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)

On Tue, Aug 18, 2026 at 3:47 PM Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com> wrote:
> Thanks Ava — this is super helpful. I'm attaching a 12-minute trace (high-traffic window from last promo) that includes per-second QPS and payload sizes.
> 
> Two quick clarifications: 
> - The 600 QPS spikes are usually ~30–40s, but we saw one ~90s spike last quarter.
> - Security: our security team will push for a VPC/private option for production, but for the pilot they are willing to accept hosted endpoints with additional audit logging.

Thanks Tom — that spike pattern is exactly the kind of signal we need.

Ben — can you take point on a quick sketch: 1) estimated monthly delta (hosted vs small dedicated pool for a 2-week pilot), and 2) a short note on whether the 90s spike changes the recommendation (i.e., if a single 90s event is a hard constraint to require Dedicated)?

Neha — I'll loop you on SLO mapping and which telemetry points we should include in the pilot agreement.

I'll schedule a short checkpoint call next week to review bench numbers.

—
Ava

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Tom Rivera <tom@opsly.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 17:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted vs Dedicated — latency & burst readiness (Opsly)

Thanks for the context — I've attached a short cost sketch (dedicated-cost-estimate.xlsx) with two scenarios:
- Scenario A (hosted-first): use Hosted API with autoscaling + burst smoothing; expected pilot cost ~ $3.2k for the 2-week window (higher per-token unit).
- Scenario B (dedicated pilot): reserve a small gpool for the pilot with burst headroom; expected pilot cost ~ $7.8k for the 2-week window (lower unit cost, predictable latency).

Observation on the 90s spike: a single 90s event won't automatically force Dedicated, but if you expect repeats (>=3 events/day) or need hard p99 guarantees during promos then Dedicated is the safer option. For the pilot we often recommend starting on Hosted with strict routing (rate limits + a transparent fallback to a slightly lower-capacity model) so you can get live traffic and validate behavior; if bench numbers or security gating require isolation we can flip to a Dedicated pilot quickly.

Next steps from my side: I will confirm the dedicated pilot pricing and include a short appendix that outlines how we handle rapid spikes (auto-burst policies, fallback model variants).

—
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0189
Attachments: dedicated-cost-estimate.xlsx (XLSX)

Subject: Supplier activation failing on mobile

Hi Ava,

We tried to complete the supplier activation link you sent this morning but the button on the activation page doesn't appear on our mobile webview (Android Chrome). Tapping the blank area does nothing. I attached a screenshot.

We can complete from desktop for now, but our AP team mostly works from tablets while on site and this will block our PO acknowledgements later this week. Can you resend a link or suggest a workaround?

Thanks,
Thomas Rey
Head of AP, Lumen Fabrications
onboarding@lumenfab.com
Attachment: screenshot-cta-hidden.png (image/png, 248 KB)


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
To: Thomas Rey <onboarding@lumenfab.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 10:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Supplier activation failing on mobile

Hi Thomas — thanks for the heads up and the screenshot.

Short answer: we saw a small subset of activation links render an empty CTA on certain Android WebViews when the deep-link parameter string exceeded ~1200 characters. That happens when a long return_url + campaign tracking + marketplace params are included. We pushed a mitigation last week but it looks like your link still had a long param set.

Workarounds:
- Open the link in a desktop browser (what you noted)
- Long-term: I'll reissue a trimmed activation link (no campaign params) and paste the direct deep-link token in the email body so mobile clients don't drop the button.

I'll re-send now and loop in Ben for an ops follow-up to ensure our link generator strips non-essential tracking for supplier activation flows.

Ava Chen
Enterprise Onboarding (Redwood Inference)
--
On Sat, Aug 15, 2026 at 09:37 AM Thomas Rey <onboarding@lumenfab.com> wrote:
> Hi Ava,
> 
> We tried to complete the supplier activation link you sent this morning but the button on the activation page doesn't appear on our mobile webview (Android Chrome).
> 
> Thanks,
> Thomas


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.ai>
Cc: Thomas Rey <onboarding@lumenfab.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 08:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Supplier activation failing on mobile

Hi team,

I dug into the generator and confirmed the issue: the link builder appended both hb_campaign_id and ga_client_id for marketing attribution. On small-screen WebViews the layout engine treated the long querystring as part of the inline button rendering, causing the CTA to flow off-canvas.

Planned fixes:
1) Short-term patch to strip non-required query params for activation emails (deploy tomorrow morning).
2) Telemetry: add a hit to capture activation page client type and render errors so we can proactively detect regressions.

Ava — can you resend the trimmed activation link to Thomas now? I'll open a ticket (SUP-8123) to track the patch and include a regression test against Android embedded WebView flows.

Ben
Customer Ops
Attachment: onboarding-instructions.pdf (application/pdf, 112 KB)

title: Founder bundle credit allocation & ops path
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Liam O'Neal <liam@launchlabs.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Founder bundle credit allocation & ops path

Hi Liam — great to meet last week. Per our call, attaching the Founder Bundle term sheet and our current pricing sheet so your ops and finance folks can see the mechanics. Quick summary of what LaunchLabs asked for and what we're proposing: 

- Ask: split $50k in promotional credits across sandbox/onboarding (for mentor teams + demos) and production pilot usage; want ability to convert leftover credits to a Dedicated commitment later.
- Proposal (recommended): 60% sandbox/onboarding (to maximize demo velocity) + 40% pilot/production; credits usable against hosted API and convertible to a prorated Dedicated credit within 9 months; credits expire 12 months from activation.

Two commercial options we can offer: 
1) Standard allocation (no additional discount) — credits applied to usage until exhaustion.
2) Bundled onboarding (recommended for LaunchLabs): 50/50 split, + 10% applied discount on the first 3 months of paid usage after credits are exhausted, and a conversion credit to Dedicated if they convert within 9 months (conversion formula: remaining credit value * 0.85).

Operational asks for your team: can contracts@launchlabs.io accept the conversion language into the addendum (we can produce a short DocuSign-ready addendum), and do you have a preferred invoice path (po number or marketplace)?

I've attached a redlined term sheet (LaunchLabs_FounderBundle_v2.pdf) and our pricing worksheet. If you want, we can do a 20-min ops call with your finance on Thursday.

Next steps: confirm preferred split and whether conversion-to-Dedicated is acceptable; I’ll loop in legal for an addendum draft.

Thanks —
Ava
Ava Chen
Growth Sales
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com
+1 (415) 555-0198

Attachments: LaunchLabs_FounderBundle_v2.pdf (term sheet, 3 pages), redwood-pricing-sheet-2026.xlsx (pricing workbook)

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: contracts@launchlabs.io
Date: Tue, 11 Aug 2026 09:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Founder bundle credit allocation & ops path

Thanks, Ava — appreciate the clear options and the docs. A few quick clarifying Qs from our side before we pull finance/legal in: 

1) Private/on-prem: can any portion of the credits be applied to a Private deployment (we may need to run a small on-prem pilot for our regulated cohort)?
2) Expiry: you mentioned 12 months from activation — is there flexibility to extend to 18 months for strategic partners that have longer procurement cycles?
3) Invoice path: we prefer PO + standard net-30 invoice, but our marketplace procurement team (cc’d) also asked whether marketplace billing (AWS Marketplace) is possible for credits conversion.

If the conversion-to-Dedicated formula can be circulated as a short addendum, contracts can review quickly. Happy to take the 20-min ops call — Thursday at 10am ET works for us.

Liam
Liam O'Neal
Partnerships Lead, LaunchLabs
liam@launchlabs.io


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 12 Aug 2026 10:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Founder bundle credit allocation & ops path

Ben / Marissa — forwarding LaunchLabs’ follow-up so we can lock commercial and ops. Key asks: private/on-prem applicability, expiry flexibility to 18 months, and invoice path PO vs marketplace. My recommendations below.

Recommendation (commercial + ops):
- Allocation: go with the Bundled onboarding offer but tweak to 55% sandbox / 45% pilot. Rationale: preserves demo velocity while giving LaunchLabs confidence they can run meaningful pilots.
- Private deployments: allow up to $10k of the credit pool to be earmarked for Private (on-prem/VPC) pilot usage. This must be explicitly tracked in billing and flagged on invoices.
- Expiry: extend to 15 months (compromise) for strategic partners; if they need 18 months, require an executive sponsor sign-off and log it on the deal.

title: SLA template: uptime guarantee and monthly remedy formula (Synapse)
preview:
From: Dave Allen <dave.allen@synapse.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Synapse Legal <legal@synapse.com>
Date: Fri, 7 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Jordan —

Thanks for sending the initial template. Attached is Synapse's procurement markup and a short list of questions we need clarified before procurement can move to PO. High-level items:

1) Uptime: We're asking for a 99.95% monthly availability commitment for our production cluster (multi-AZ).
2) Remedy: Synapse prefers a pro-rata monthly credit tied to % downtime rather than a fixed cap; they want clear calculation examples.
3) Maintenance: we need a defined maintenance carve-out + a 72-hour advance notice requirement.
4) Regional failover: how will cross-region failovers count toward SLA?

I've attached Synapse_marked_up_SLA.pdf for reference. Can you confirm Redwood's standard stance and whether the remedy calculation below is negotiable?

Synapse proposed remedy (excerpt):
- If monthly availability >= 99.95%: no credit
- 99.90%–99.94%: 5% credit of monthly invoice
- 99.0%–99.89%: 15% credit
- <99.0%: 30% credit

Thanks — happy to hop on a quick call if needed.

Best,
Dave
Procurement Manager | Synapse Systems

Attachment: Synapse_marked_up_SLA.pdf (pdf)


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 7 Aug 2026 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Team — forwarding Synapse's markup and asks. They're pushing for 99.95% availability and a pro-rata credit ladder (see Dave's email). Key negotiation points I want input on:

- Can we accept 99.95% for multi-AZ production in their region (us-west-2) without a major price uplift?
- Remedy structure: their ladder is steeper than our standard. What's the nearest equivalent we can offer that preserves a cap but gives a clearer example table?
- Maintenance and regional failover carve-outs: sync with Product/Infra guidance so we're not over-promising.

I've attached our baseline SLA draft (SLA_Synapse_v1.docx) and created a redlined PDF with my initial comments. Please add quick edits and flag any non-starters. I'm aiming to get a consolidated position back to Dave early next week.

Thanks,
Jordan
Head of Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference

Attachment: SLA_Synapse_v1.docx (docx), SLA_Synapse_v1_redlines.pdf (pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Jordan / team — my take after reading their ladder:

- 99.95% ask is doable for a committed, dedicated pool (reserved capacity) but not for hosted burst instances. We should tie 99.95% to a Dedicated contract or an uplifted rate. For hosted, 99.9% is our baseline.

- Remedy table: rather than full pro-rata, propose a hybrid: small automatic credit bands plus an annualized cap. Example:
  * >=99.95%: 0%
  * 99.90–99.949%: 3%
  * 99.00–99.899%: 10%
  * <99.00%: 25%
  Cap: monthly credits cannot exceed 50% of monthly invoice (we can propose an alternate cap if they push).

- Maintenance carve-out: we should keep a 48-hour advance notice for scheduled maintenance for service impacting events; for emergency maintenance, define rapid escalation and a postmortem within 7 business days. Synapse's 72-hour ask is fine if they're a Dedicated customer.

- Regional failover: failovers initiated by customer (e.g., their DNS changes) should not count toward Redwood's availability. Automated failover due to our regional outage should be covered.

I'll draft language for the remedy formula and the cap. Flag if you want different numbers before I join the call with Synapse.

-Ben
Sr. Commercial Manager | Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 11:27:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Ben — +1 on tying 99.95% to Dedicated. A few product/legal notes from my side after scanning the doc: 

1) We need to call out the quantization of maintenance windows (max 4 hours per maintenance event for minor upgrades) and a monthly aggregate (e.g., up to 8 hours/month scheduled maintenance that won't count as downtime).
2) For the credit calculation examples, include an explicit formula: monthly credit = (monthly invoice) * (% downtime bucket credit). Also add a worked example to avoid back-and-forth during procurement.
3) For multi-region failover: clarify that cross-region replication lag that causes degraded performance counts only if it prevents the service from responding to requests (not per-token latency spikes).

I'll add redlines to the doc and propose the hybrid ladder Ben suggested but tighten the cap to 40% for the monthly credit cap (we can negotiate upward if Synapse pushes). Also recommend we offer a one-time operational allowance (first month) if Synapse signs a 12-month Dedicated commitment to help them through cutover.

-Marissa
Director, Product Partnerships | Redwood Inference


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Dave Allen <dave.allen@synapse.com>
Cc: Synapse Legal <legal@synapse.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Dave — thanks for the clear markup and questions. We've reviewed internally and can propose the following consolidated position to keep the procurement process moving: 

1) Availability: Redwood can commit to 99.95% monthly availability for Synapse if the contract is for a Dedicated capacity pool (reserved GPU capacity) in us-west-2. For standard hosted tiers, the availability commitment remains 99.9%.

2) Remedy ladder: we recommend a hybrid ladder with worked examples. Proposed bands:
  - >=99.95%: 0% credit
  - 99.90%–99.949%: 3% of monthly invoice
  - 99.00%–99.899%: 10% of monthly invoice
  - <99.00%: 25% of monthly invoice
  Monthly credit cap: 40% of monthly invoice. (We can revisit the cap if Synapse takes a 12-month Dedicated commitment; in that case we can offer a 50% cap for the first 90 days.)

3) Maintenance carve-out: scheduled maintenance (announced >=48 hours in advance) will be excluded from uptime calculations. We will limit scheduled maintenance to a maximum of 8 hours per month aggregate for production-impacting events and 4 hours max per event for minor upgrades. Emergency maintenance will be documented with a postmortem shared within 7 business days.

4) Regional failover: if Redwood initiates automated failover due to a regional outage, the time during failover will be counted as downtime; customer-initiated failover that results from Synapse changes (DNS, routing) will not count toward Redwood's downtime. Cross-region replication lag will only count if it causes an inability to serve requests.

5) Example calculation: if Synapse's monthly invoice is $100,000 and measured availability for August is 99.85% (downtime bucket 99.00–99.899%), the monthly credit would be 10% -> $10,000, subject to the 40% monthly cap.

Next steps: attached is a redlined SLA draft with the above edits (SLA_Synapse_v1_redlines.pdf) and a versioned doc (SLA_Synapse_v2.docx). Please review and let me know if you'd like a 30-minute call to walk through the bands and cap. If Synapse prefers the original pro-rata ladder, we can discuss trading on a committed volume/term to get closer to their values.

If legal prefers different phrasing for carve-outs or notices, loop them in and we'll iterate quickly.

Thanks — looking forward to finalizing.

Jordan Blake
Head of Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference

Attachments: SLA_Synapse_v1_redlines.pdf (pdf), SLA_Synapse_v2.docx (docx)

title: Clarifying procurement options for FinSecure — marketplace or enterprise agreement
preview:
From: Alicia Vega <alicia.vega@finsecure.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@finsecure.com, Maya Singh <maya.singh@finsecure.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: FinSecure RFP — clarification on marketplace vs enterprise purchase route

Hi Alex,

Thanks for taking our call last week. I've attached our RFP and a short pricing questionnaire — the procurement team is trying to map out the buying lanes and we need a clear summary from Redwood on whether we should pursue: (A) a marketplace private offer (AWS Marketplace / GCP Marketplace / Azure Private Offer) or (B) a direct enterprise procurement with an MSA and invoiced PO.

Specific questions we need addressed for our finance and security teams:
- Which route gives us the fastest procurement-to-production timeline for a reserved capacity pilot?
- Are there differences in seller-of-record and tax implications across marketplaces vs direct invoicing?
- Any constraints on CO-SELL credits or joint-sales eligibility if we go marketplace?
- How do subscription terms, termination, and data residency controls differ?
- Typical lead times for private-offer setup vs MSA negotiation at your end.

We attached the RFP and a short list of procurement questions. Can you respond with a brief pros/cons matrix and recommended path for a regulated fintech? We're hoping to get internal approval this month.

Thanks — happy to set a follow-up call once you've reviewed.

Best,
Alicia Vega
Head of Procurement | FinSecure
procurement@finsecure.com

Attachments: FinSecure_RFP.pdf; finsecure_pricing_questions.xlsx


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Vega <alicia.vega@finsecure.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2026 11:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FinSecure RFP — clarification on marketplace vs enterprise purchase route

Hi Alicia —

Thanks for sending the RFP and the questionnaire. I reviewed the attachments and pulled together an initial summary (attached) that highlights the main tradeoffs for a regulated fintech like FinSecure. High level: both routes can work; the right one depends on your procurement constraints and priority between speed and long-term billing/control.

Quick summary (pros/cons):
- Marketplace (private offer):
  * Pros: faster procurement turnaround (private offers can be issued in days once marketplace account setup is complete), predictable cloud-native billing, easier PO mapping in some finance systems.
  * Cons: seller-of-record depends on the marketplace and listing (AWS/GCP/Azure have different models), possible limitations on co-sell incentives unless we register the opportunity early, and sometimes less flexibility on contract language/termination terms.
- Direct enterprise contract (MSA + invoiced PO):
  * Pros: full control over legal terms, easier to negotiate SLAs, data residency language, and billing cadence (Net30/Net45). Co-sell and channel crediting is straightforward to document in our partner processes.
  * Cons: longer negotiation (MSA + SOW + PO) — typically 2–6 weeks for standard cases; more manual invoicing operationally.

Immediate recommendation: If your gating factor is speed to pilot and you can accept the marketplace seller-of-record model, pursue a private offer on your preferred cloud (we can fast-track with our marketplace contacts). If you need bespoke legal terms or special data residency clauses for the pilot, we should do a direct MSA and invoice route.

I've attached a short one-page matrix and a proposed agenda for a 30-minute procurement alignment call. Lauren — can you add any notes about co-sell registration windows for AWS/Azure/GCP? Ben — can you confirm lead times for private-offer activation with our marketplace ops team?

If it's useful, I can be on a call with you and your procurement team tomorrow or Wednesday afternoon.

Best,

Alex Martinez
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com | +1 415-555-0123

Attachments: alex_summary_marketplace_vs_msa.pdf


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Vega <alicia.vega@finsecure.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2026 13:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FinSecure RFP — clarification on marketplace vs enterprise purchase route

Hi Alicia — quick follow-up from my side with a few practical procurement bullets to consider:

- Co-sell: For AWS and Azure, co-sell eligibility typically requires deal registration before contract signature and submission of a partner intake form. For GCP, co-sell is possible but we usually coordinate with the partner manager earlier in the engagement. If co-sell credits matter for your internal incentives, we should register the opportunity when we have a scoped pilot.
- Seller-of-record: AWS Marketplace Private Offer invoices are often issued by the listing partner (seller) or the marketplace, depending on how the private offer is configured. That can affect tax handling and your AP workflows; our finance ops team can provide a mapping per cloud.
- Billing cadence & PO: If you need consolidated monthly invoicing to a single vendor with specific tax IDs, direct MSA + invoicing is simpler. Marketplaces sometimes issue multiple line items per cloud account.
- Security & compliance: On private offers we can still commit to the same data handling and audit logs, but some enterprise-specific language (e.g., audit right clauses, specific breach notification timelines) sometimes only live comfortably in an MSA addendum.

Next steps I suggest:
1) Quick 30-min call this week with procurement + our finance ops to decide whether to request a marketplace private offer or start MSA review.

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Vega <alicia.vega@finsecure.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2026 11:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FinSecure RFP — clarification on marketplace vs enterprise purchase route

Hi Alicia —

Thanks for sending the RFP and the questionnaire. I reviewed the attachments and pulled together an initial summary (attached) that highlights the main tradeoffs for a regulated fintech like FinSecure. High level: both routes can work; the right one depends on your procurement constraints and priority between speed and long-term billing/control.

Quick summary (pros/cons):
- Marketplace (private offer):
  * Pros: faster procurement turnaround (private offers can be issued in days once marketplace account setup is complete), predictable cloud-native billing, easier PO mapping in some finance systems.
  * Cons: seller-of-record depends on the marketplace and listing (AWS/GCP/Azure have different models), possible limitations on co-sell incentives unless we register the opportunity early, and sometimes less flexibility on contract language/termination terms.
- Direct enterprise contract (MSA + invoiced PO):
  * Pros: full control over legal terms, easier to negotiate SLAs, data residency language, and billing cadence (Net30/Net45). Co-sell and channel crediting is straightforward to document in our partner processes.
  * Cons: longer negotiation (MSA + SOW + PO) — typically 2–6 weeks for standard cases; more manual invoicing operationally.

Immediate recommendation: If your gating factor is speed to pilot and you can accept the marketplace seller-of-record model, pursue a private offer on your preferred cloud (we can fast-track with our marketplace contacts). If you need bespoke legal terms or special data residency clauses for the pilot, we should do a direct MSA and invoice route.

I've attached a short one-page matrix and a proposed agenda for a 30-minute procurement alignment call. Lauren — can you add any notes about co-sell registration windows for AWS/Azure/GCP? Ben — can you confirm lead times for private-offer activation with our marketplace ops team?

If it's useful, I can be on a call with you and your procurement team tomorrow or Wednesday afternoon.

Best,

Alex Martinez

Subject: Re: FinSecure RFP — clarification on marketplace vs enterprise purchase route

Hi Alicia — quick follow-up from my side with a few practical procurement bullets to consider:

- Co-sell: For AWS and Azure, co-sell eligibility typically requires deal registration before contract signature and submission of a partner intake form. For GCP, co-sell is possible but we usually coordinate with the partner manager earlier in the engagement. If co-sell credits matter for your internal incentives, we should register the opportunity when we have a scoped pilot.
- Seller-of-record: AWS Marketplace Private Offer invoices are often issued by the listing partner (seller) or the marketplace, depending on how the private offer is configured. That can affect tax handling and your AP workflows; our finance ops team can provide a mapping per cloud.
- Billing cadence & PO: If you need consolidated monthly invoicing to a single vendor with specific tax IDs, direct MSA + invoicing is simpler. Marketplaces sometimes issue multiple line items per cloud account.
- Security & compliance: On private offers we can still commit to the same data handling and audit logs, but some enterprise-specific language (e.g., audit right clauses, specific breach notification timelines) sometimes only live comfortably in an MSA addendum.

Next steps I suggest:
1) Quick 30-min call this week with procurement + our finance ops to decide whether to request a marketplace private offer or start MSA review.

Hi Samantha — attaching the pricing one-pager and a 1-page SOW draft. Below is a compact table you can paste into procurement: 

Redwood example pricing (net examples, illustrative):
- Tier A (pilot): 0–50M tokens/mo — list $150 per 1M; discounted $120 per 1M with a 3-month pilot credit
- Tier B (initial production): 50–300M tokens/mo — list $120 per 1M; committed 3mo+ volume discount to $80–$65 per 1M depending on exact band
- Tier C (scale): 300M–800M tokens/mo — committed capacity pricing; effective $60 per 1M at 500M+

Notes:
- Hosted API: flexible month-to-month billing; discounts apply with 3+ month commitments.
- Dedicated (Reserved): requires minimum 6–12 month commit; pricing materially better at scale but requires infra onboarding and SOW + invoice cadence.
- Billing/POs: we accept standard POs under $15k/mo; for ongoing monthly invoices above that threshold procurement review + an executed order form is required.

SOW draft maps pilot (4–8 weeks) and production milestone (enablement, baseline SLA). I attached: Redwood-pricing-guide-2026.pdf and SOW-draft-orchard-20260802.docx. Also included the W-9 and SOC 2 summary links in the one-pager.

If agreement looks fine to procurement, options:
  A) Start Hosted pilot under PO (if < $15k/mo) — fastest to go live.
  B) Contract + monthly invoices for > $15k/mo with a 30–60 day procurement lead.
  C) Reserved capacity (Dedicated) if you want predictable unit economics at >$60k/mo and are comfortable with 6–12 month commits.

Happy to join the procurement sync on Thursday. 

— Marissa
